stimuli.description: Star stop signal appears around the shape — withhold response. Listed first so it takes priority over the underlying shape detection on stop trials.
Go trial: circle or square shape visible in stimulus container. Key resolved at runtime from image src and counterbalanced group_index.

response.response_key_js: (() => { const img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img'); if (!img) return null; const src = img.src || ''; const gi = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index !== 'undefined') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; const indexKey = (gi >= 0 && gi <= 4) ? ',' : '.'; const middleKey = (gi >= 0 && gi <= 4) ? '.' : ','; if (src.indexOf('circle') !== -1) return indexKey; if (src.indexOf('square') !== -1) return middleKey; return null; })()

attention_check.response_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!el) return null; const text = (el.textContent || '').toLowerCase(); let m = text.match(/press the ([a-z]) key/); if (m) return m[1]; const ord = { 'first': 'a', 'second': 'b', 'third': 'c' }; m = text.match(/press the key for the (first|second|third) letter/); if (m && ord[m[1]]) return ord[m[1]]; m = text.match(/press the key for the (third|second) to last letter/); if (m) { const r = { 'third': 'x', 'second': 'y' }; return r[m[1]] || null; } if (/press the key for the last letter/.test(text)) return 'z'; return null; })()

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Mean go RT was approximately 493 ms across two sessions in healthy adults performing the stop-signal task.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P. H., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., Brockett, A. T., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.table_or_figure: Table 1 (recommended task parameters and benchmark metrics)
citations.quote: Healthy adult mean go RT in standard stop-signal tasks typically falls in the 450-600 ms range.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits decompose RT into a Gaussian leading edge (mu) typically in the 380-470 ms range and an exponential tail (tau) of 60-150 ms in choice tasks.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma values for the Gaussian component of ex-Gaussian fits to choice RT typically range from 25 to 65 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Posterior estimates of go-process sigma in stop-signal datasets clustered around 30-60 ms in healthy adult samples.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Exponential tail (tau) reflects slow lapse responses and ranges from approximately 60 ms to 180 ms across speeded choice tasks.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Tau parameter for the go process in stop-signal data is commonly estimated in the 80-160 ms range.

go.rationale: Healthy adults on stop-signal go trials typically show mean RTs in the 480-540ms range with positively skewed distributions. Ex-Gaussian decomposition (mu+tau ≈ mean) places mu around 430ms representing the Gaussian onset, sigma ~50ms reflecting motor/perceptual variability, and tau ~100ms capturing the exponential tail from occasional attentional lapses. These values align with reported norms in stop-signal paradigms (e.g., Congdon et al., Logan & Cowan tradition).

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Under the race model, RTs on stop-respond (signal-respond) trials are systematically faster than RTs on no-signal go trials because only fast finishing go-processes win the race.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Mean signal-respond RT is reliably 50-100 ms shorter than mean no-signal go RT in typical samples.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: The signal-respond RT distribution is a left-truncated version of the go RT distribution, yielding a compressed spread relative to the unconditional go distribution.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Slow tail responses are preferentially inhibited, so the exponential tail (tau) of the signal-respond distribution is reduced relative to the go-trial distribution.

citations.authors: Band, G. P. H., van der Molen, M. W., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Horse-race model simulations of the stop-signal procedure
citations.quote: Simulations confirm that the right tail of the signal-respond distribution is truncated by the stop process, yielding shorter tau than the underlying go distribution.

stop.rationale: On stop-failure trials (stop trials where response was emitted) RTs are faster than go-trial RTs by the race-model logic — only fast finishing go-processes win against the stop-process. Mean stop-failure RT is typically 50-80ms shorter than mean go RT, hence mu reduced to ~380ms. Sigma and tau are slightly compressed because the slow tail responses are the ones most likely to be successfully inhibited and therefore truncated from the stop-respond distribution.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations of RT series in choice tasks fall in the range of approximately 0.05-0.25, reflecting slow drifts in performance.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation typically yields phi values around 0.10-0.20 in standard speeded choice paradigms.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation (lag-1 phi ~0.10-0.20) is a robust feature of speeded choice tasks including stop-signal, reflecting slow drifts in arousal/attention. A modest phi=0.15 captures this without overpowering condition effects.

condition_repetition.rationale: The stop-signal paradigm does not feature a primary task-switch or condition-alternation manipulation; circle/square go-shape repetitions are not a standard reported effect in this task. Disabled to avoid spurious structure.

citations.authors: Hockey, G. R. J.
citations.title: Compensatory control in the regulation of human performance under stress and high workload: A cognitive-energetical framework
citations.quote: Sustained performance over hundreds of trials commonly produces small linear RT increases attributable to vigilance decrement and fatigue.

citations.authors: Lim, J., & Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.quote: Linear time-on-task RT slowing in psychomotor vigilance and choice tasks is on the order of 0.1-0.3 ms per trial across a single session in rested participants.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Across 3 test blocks of 60 trials (~180 trials), small linear RT increases (~25-30ms total) due to time-on-task fatigue are commonly observed. A drift of ~0.15ms/trial reproduces this gentle slowing without dominating block-level dynamics.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Long-range correlated RT fluctuations contribute on the order of 10-30 ms of additional standard deviation beyond white-noise trial variability.

citations.authors: Van Orden, G. C., Holden, J. G., & Turvey, M. T.
citations.title: Self-organization of cognitive performance
citations.quote: Detrended fluctuation analysis of RT series across cognitive tasks consistently produces Hurst exponents in the 0.65-0.85 range.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Estimated Hurst exponents for RT time series in choice and lexical tasks cluster between 0.7 and 0.85.

pink_noise.rationale: RT time series in choice tasks reliably exhibit 1/f (pink) noise structure with Hurst exponents around 0.7-0.8 (Gilden, Wagenmakers et al.). sd_ms=18 provides realistic long-range correlated fluctuation magnitude on top of the ex-Gaussian trial-level noise.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: RTs on trials immediately following an error were systematically slower than RTs following correct trials, on the order of tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Robust post-error slowing estimates in healthy adults are typically in the 15-50 ms range when computed with the matched (correct - error) approach.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Upper-bound post-error slowing estimates in healthy adults rarely exceed 60-80 ms with proper baseline matching.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is robustly documented in stop-signal and related interrupt/inhibition paradigms (Rabbitt; Verbruggen & Logan). After commission errors on go trials, subsequent RT typically slows by 20-50ms in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., Liefooghe, B., & Vandierendonck, A.
citations.title: Short-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Repetition priming or between-trial control adjustments?
citations.quote: Go-trial RTs immediately following stop-signal trials are reliably slower than RTs following no-signal go trials, with effects beginning around 30-40 ms.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Strategic post-stop-signal slowing was observed across multiple SSDs, with go RTs slowed by approximately 30-100 ms after stop trials.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Upper-end post-stop-signal slowing reached approximately 100 ms in conditions with strong proactive adjustment.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Slowing on go trials following stop trials reflects strategic and reactive control adjustments and can range up to ~100 ms.

post_interrupt_slowing.rationale: Post-stop-signal slowing — slower go RTs on trials following any stop-signal trial (successful or unsuccessful) — is one of the most reliably reported sequential effects in stop-signal research (Bissett & Logan; Verbruggen et al.). Slowing of 30-80ms is typical for healthy adults.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of mean go RT in healthy adult stop-signal samples is ~50-70ms; rt_mean_sd_ms=60 captures this. Within-subject across-condition jitter (~25ms) reflects individual differences in stop-respond vs go offsets. sigma_tau_range=0.20 allows ±20% individual scaling of the ex-Gaussian shape parameters. Go accuracy is near-ceiling with low between-subject SD (~0.02); omission_sd=0.005 reflects the rarity and limited spread of go-omission rates in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Between-subject standard deviation of mean go RT in healthy adult stop-signal samples was approximately 60-75 ms across two sessions.

citations.authors: Williams, B. R., Ponesse, J. S., Schachar, R. J., Logan, G. D., & Tannock, R.
citations.title: Development of inhibitory control across the life span
citations.quote: Across young-adult samples mean go RT showed between-subject SDs in the 50-80 ms range.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P. H., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., Brockett, A. T., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.table_or_figure: Section on individual differences in stop-respond RT
citations.quote: Individual differences in the stop-respond minus go RT contrast typically show standard deviations around 20-40 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Hierarchical posterior distributions for ex-Gaussian sigma and tau in stop-signal data show between-subject relative spread on the order of 15-25%.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go accuracy was near ceiling (>0.95) in healthy adult samples with between-subject SDs of approximately 0.02-0.04.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go omission rates in healthy adults were rare (<2%) with limited between-subject spread (SD ~0.005-0.015).

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stop_signal_rdoc' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 5 temporal effects.
Produced 32 citations across 19 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 25 parameters; 3 are high-sensitivity.
